subject: The Island of California — the cartographic error, current c.1622–1770s, by which California was drawn as an island severed from North America. It spread not by many independent mistakes but by copying: cartographers of the period routinely copied prior maps, so one misreading propagated down a lineage of copies and outlived repeated first-hand proof to the contrary (Eusebio Kino, overland, 1698–1706, peninsula map 1701; Fernando Consag, by sea to the head of the Gulf, 1746). Sources cited in the work.
note: The base landmass is a SCHEMATIC of the copying mechanism, not a reproduction of any real historical map and not a claim about real geography. Every drifted shape here is generated live by a seeded random walk (mulberry32); no historical map is copied or invented. 'Same seed → same work'; the recopy control advances the seed to expose the system's range, including outputs where the strait, by chance, barely opens.
